summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Right big toe pain and redness.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports a two-week history of intermittent right big toe pain, which has become more severe and painful last week. He experienced significant pain and inability to walk at a trade show convention. The pain is described as throbbing, occasionally sharp, and worsens with movement or prolonged walking. He reports walking on his heel to avoid bending the toe. He has been taking ibuprofen twice daily with limited relief. He denies any recent injury or trauma. He reports feeling a bone spur and some grinding in the joint. The patient reports that the sheets bother the toe when he is in bed at night.

Findings
Examination reveals a large spur on the top of the right big toe joint and evidence of cartilage loss. The patient reports feeling bone spurs and a grinding sensation in the joint upon palpation and range of motion testing. The patient reports that the sheets bother the toe when he is in bed at night. The patient reports that the pain is exacerbated by pushing in on the joint and moving it back and forth. The patient reports feeling pain when the toe is flexed to 20 degrees of plantar flexion.

Assessment
The patient's symptoms and physical exam findings are consistent with hallux rigidus with significant cartilage loss and a large bony spur, resulting in arthritis of the right big toe joint.

Plan
We will initiate treatment with an orthotic insert for the patient's shoe and prescribe meloxicam for anti-inflammatory effects, to be taken once daily. We will schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to assess the patient's response to treatment. If significant improvement is not observed, we will discuss more invasive options, such as a joint injection or surgery to clean out the joint. We will also advise the patient to continue with his regular activities, including fishing, and to monitor for any worsening of symptoms or the onset of stomach upset. The patient should contact us if he experiences any heartburn or stomach pain.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right great toe pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Joseph Walker is a pleasant 58-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of right great toe pain. The onset of his pain began 2 weeks ago, however it worsened last week. He noticed the pain worsening when he was at a trade show convention and he could not ambulate as he was forced to stand there as the pain was there the whole time. He denies any specific injury. The patient describes his pain usually as throbbing and burning, but notes it occasionally changes to sharp, stabbing pain especially with movement or prolonged ambulation. His symptoms also include redness to the right great toe. The patient states that he has been ambulating on his heel to keep his toe from bending. He reports that his pain is present even with the slightest of pressure, which he notes is worse at night when his sheet is touching his right toe. He adds that he has been taking 2 ibuprofen per day, which does not provide him with relief.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient reports that he likes to bicycle ride.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right great toe pain.
Skin: Reports redness.

PHYSICAL EXAM

MSK: Examination of the right great toe reveals 10 degrees of plantar flexion with pain. Pain to palpation of the right great toe, between the big toe and 2nd toe. Palpated a bone spur on the right great toe.

RESULTS

X-ray of the right great toe taken today in office reveals a large bone spur on the anterior aspect of the right great toe joint. There is a loss of cartilage with some arthritis present.

ASSESSMENT

Right foot hallux rigidus.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to his current symptoms. I have explained to him that his x-rays revealed hallux rigidus. We discussed treatment options for this and I have recommended that we begin with conservative treatment in the form of custom orthotics. I have also prescribed the patient meloxicam once a day to treat the pain. The patient was instructed to discontinue use and contact the office if gastrointestinal issues develop. I advised the patient that I want him to continue his regular activities.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me in 2 weeks to check on his progress. If his pain does not improve with the orthotics, I will recommend a cortisone injection or surgical intervention.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports feeling bone spurs and a grinding sensation in the joint upon palpation and range of motion testing.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This sentence is framed as patient-reported sensation rather than examiner-observed findings; however, it is closely tied to the exam maneuver and does not substantially mislead.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports that the sheets bother the toe when he is in bed at night.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a subjective symptom description and belongs in the history rather than the objective findings section, but its duplication here is unlikely to confuse clinical interpretation.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports that the pain is exacerbated by pushing in on the joint and moving it back and forth.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This describes symptom provocation as experienced by the patient, fitting HPI; in Findings it is slightly misleading but still understandable in context of exam maneuvers.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports feeling pain when the toe is flexed to 20 degrees of plantar flexion.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a subjective pain response rather than an objective measurement; while linked to an exam position, it is more appropriately part of the symptom history.
